TextENGNew: This piece alone is terribly warped.
changeReason: Old: 'This piece is the only one that's so distorted' vs New: 'This piece alone is terribly warped.' The old implies uniqueness among pieces while the new emphasizes the degree of warping; 'distorted' vs 'warped' shifts the nature of the abnormality.

TextENGNew: Because it comes from long before June of 1983.
Therefore, whether this piece exists or not has no bearing whatsoever on the battle we wage on this game board.
changeReason: Old frames it as 'existence of this piece has no affect' (passive/abstract) while new says 'whether this piece exists or not has no bearing on the battle we wage on this game board' — specifies 'battle' and 'game board' separately, matching TextJPN's 'ゲーム盤の戦い' more precisely.

TextENGNew: ...But is it truly a useless piece with no bearing at all?
changeReason: Old simply asks 'But is this really a useless piece?' while new adds 'with no bearing at all?' preserving the TextJPN's full phrase '何の影響も及ぼさない役立たずのカケラ' — the qualifier about having no influence is significant.

TextENGNew: By now, the true nature of Rule Y has been completely laid bare.
Backed by a vast and powerful organization, it is strong enough to swallow whole the fragile bonds between individuals.
changeReason: Old says 'swallow up the small solidarity of individuals' while new says 'swallow whole the fragile bonds between individuals.' 'Solidarity' (collective unity) vs 'bonds' (personal connections) conveys a different understanding of what is threatened.

TextENGNew: Its power is so utterly unreasonable that I have nearly given up on this game time and again.
changeReason: Old: 'I almost give up' (present/single instance) vs New: 'I have nearly given up on this game time and again' — the new explicitly conveys repeated near-surrender ('何度も'), a significant narrative detail about the speaker's ongoing struggle.

TextENGNew: And yet, that is precisely why I find myself thinking...
...that perhaps, hidden within this piece, there sleeps a game piece capable of standing against so formidable a foe.
changeReason: Old says 'a piece hidden somewhere that's strong enough to fight' while new says 'hidden within this piece, there sleeps a game piece capable of standing against.' The new specifies the piece is hidden *within this specific fragment*, matching TextJPN's 'このカケラの中に'.

TextENGNew: Mamoru Akasaka works at the Metropolitan Police Department in Tokyo, and he may well possess the power to stand against a conspiracy of that magnitude.
changeReason: Old: 'police headquarters' vs New: 'Metropolitan Police Department' — the new is a more precise translation of '警視庁' (which is specifically the Tokyo Metropolitan Police, not a generic headquarters). Also 'might have the power' vs 'may well possess the power' shifts certainty slightly.

TextENGNew: His strength — I believe — will reach even Rule Y.
changeReason: Old converts a confident statement (きっと及ぶ, 'surely will reach') into a question expressing doubt ('Can his power withstand even Rule Y?'). Reverses the emotional tone.

TextENGNew: Just as Keiichi Maebara shattered Rule Z so brilliantly, might Akasaka shatter Rule Y in the same fashion?
changeReason: Old: 'will he be able to defeat Rule Y so easily?' (questioning/doubtful) vs New: 'might Akasaka shatter Rule Y in the same fashion?' — The new names Akasaka explicitly and uses 'shatter' matching '打ち破る'. Both are questions but the old implies ease ('so easily') while the new asks about manner ('in the same fashion').

TextENGNew: Akasaka's strength, too — it is indispensable...
